content:
https://rust.docs.kernel.org/kernel/miscdevice/trait.MiscDevice.html

Hi folks, I am reading about the ioctl method in the trait linked above. I am reading through the sample code too. Is the idea that any data I want to send through to the device module can be serialized into an integer and sent through the ioctl call in the arg argument? What if i want to send more data than what fits inside a usize? Is there a better interface for sending and receiving data across the device boundary? It would be nice if i could call read and write on the device.

Is the idea that any data I want to send through to the device module can be serialized into an integer and sent through the ioctl call in the arg argument?

You could do this by treating all user-space data as integers, but this is clearly unsafe and violates the specifications. For kernel drivers, all inputs originating from user space are inherently unsafe and untrusted, The standard practice is to use the ​Untrusted Data API, please see https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250421134909.464405-1-benno.lossin@proton.me/

What if i want to send more data than what fits inside a usize?

It can, please refer to the documentation of UserSlice.

And read, write interface you can refer to https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250311-iov-iter-v1-0-f6c9134ea824@google.com/

JCKeep said:

The standard practice is to use the ​Untrusted Data API, please see https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250421134909.464405-1-benno.lossin@proton.me/

That API is experimental not yet merged, I would wait until it's finished.

Benno Lossin said:

JCKeep said:

The standard practice is to use the ​Untrusted Data API, please see https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20250421134909.464405-1-benno.lossin@proton.me/

That API is experimental not yet merged, I would wait until it's finished.

Yeah, this is an extremely complex task, balancing safety and usability while grappling with Rust's semantics.
It's quite common for ioctls to interpret the ioctl argument as a pointer into userspace and read or write data to userspace via kernel::uaccess.
